questions.question: What is the recommended project structure for a TypeScript application?
options: A) All files in the root directory
B) Source code in src/, compiled output in dist/, tests alongside or in a tests/ folder
C) One giant file with all code
D) Separate folders for each function

questions.question: Why should you separate source code (src/) from build output (dist/)?
options: A) It's required by TypeScript
B) To keep generated files out of version control and clearly distinguish source from output
C) For faster compilation
D) It doesn't matter

questions.question: Which files should typically be in .gitignore for a TypeScript project?
options: A) src/ and tsconfig.json
B) node_modules/, dist/, and coverage/
C) package.json and package-lock.json
D) All .ts files

questions.question: What is the purpose of an entry point file (e.g., src/index.ts)?
options: A) To store configuration
B) To serve as the main starting point of the application, orchestrating imports and execution
C) To export all types
D) To define test cases

questions.question: When building a CLI tool in TypeScript, what does 'process.argv' contain?
options: A) Environment variables
B) An array of command-line arguments passed to the Node.js process
C) The process ID
D) File system paths

questions.question: What are the first two elements of process.argv?
options: A) The script name and first argument
B) The path to node executable and the path to the script file
C) The username and working directory
D) The command name and version

questions.question: Why is it important to define types/interfaces for your data models?
options: A) It makes the code longer
B) It provides compile-time safety, documentation, and editor autocompletion
C) TypeScript requires it for all variables
D) It improves runtime performance

questions.question: How should modules be organized in a capstone project?
options: A) Everything in one file
B) Group related functionality into separate modules with clear responsibilities
C) One function per file, no grouping
D) Alphabetically by function name

questions.question: What is the benefit of using barrel files (index.ts) in a project?
options: A) They make the project compile faster
B) They simplify imports by providing a single entry point for each module directory
C) They are required for TypeScript to work
D) They prevent circular dependencies

questions.question: Which tsconfig.json setting is recommended for a new TypeScript project?
options: A) "strict": false for easier development
B) "strict": true to catch potential bugs early
C) No tsconfig.json needed
D) "strict": "sometimes"

questions.question: What is a good set of npm scripts for a TypeScript project?
options: A) Only "start"
B) "build" (tsc), "start" (node dist/index.js), "test" (vitest), "dev" (ts-node or watch mode)
C) Only "build" and "test"
D) No scripts needed, run commands manually

questions.question: What does the "build" script typically do in a TypeScript project?
options: A) Runs the application
B) Compiles TypeScript to JavaScript using tsc
C) Installs dependencies
D) Runs tests

questions.question: Why should you write tests for your capstone project?
options: A) Tests are optional and add no value
B) Tests verify correctness, catch regressions, and document expected behavior
C) Tests are only for production code
D) Tests make the code run faster

questions.question: What testing strategy combines unit tests, integration tests, and edge case tests?
options: A) Random testing
B) A comprehensive testing strategy that validates individual functions, module interactions, and boundary conditions
C) Only testing the main function
D) Manual testing only

questions.question: When combining types and modules, where should shared types be defined?
options: A) In every file that uses them
B) In a dedicated types file or types/ directory, exported for use across modules
C) Only in the entry point
D) In package.json

questions.question: What is the purpose of the Result type pattern in a capstone project?
options: A) To return multiple values
B) To handle errors as values in the type system instead of relying on try/catch for control flow
C) To wrap all functions in Promises
D) To create database result sets

questions.question: How should you handle external data (e.g., file input, API responses) in a TypeScript project?
options: A) Trust the data and use type assertions
B) Validate with type guards or schema validation before using the data
C) Ignore type checking for external data
D) Only accept string data

questions.question: What does 'separation of concerns' mean in project architecture?
options: A) Keeping source code and tests in different repositories
B) Organizing code so each module has a single, well-defined responsibility
C) Separating frontend and backend code
D) Using separate terminals for different tasks

questions.question: What is the benefit of using async/await with proper error handling in a capstone project?
options: A) It makes the code synchronous
B) It provides readable asynchronous code with clear error handling paths
C) It eliminates all errors
D) It is faster than callbacks

questions.question: What should you consider when choosing between 'interface' and 'type' for your data models?
options: A) Always use type, interfaces are deprecated
B) Interfaces for object shapes that may be extended; types for unions, intersections, and complex compositions
C) Always use interface, types are slower
D) It makes no difference at all

questions.question: Why is it important to have a clean 'npm run build' before deploying?
options: A) To update package.json
B) To verify the project compiles without errors and produces correct output
C) To install new dependencies
D) To format the code

questions.question: What role does tsconfig.json's 'include' and 'exclude' play in a capstone project?
options: A) They control which npm packages are installed
B) They control which files are compiled, ensuring test files or config aren't bundled into dist/
C) They have no practical effect
D) They control IDE features

questions.question: What is a common pattern for organizing CLI command handling?
options: A) One giant switch statement in index.ts
B) A commands module with separate handler functions for each command, dispatched from main
C) Separate processes for each command
D) Using eval() to parse commands

questions.question: When should you use generics in your capstone project?
options: A) Never, they're too complex
B) When you have reusable logic that works with multiple types (e.g., Result<T,E>, utility functions)
C) Only for array operations
D) Only in test files

questions.question: What is the final checklist before considering a TypeScript capstone project complete?
options: A) Just make sure it compiles
B) Code compiles (tsc), tests pass, strict mode enabled, proper project structure, error handling in place
C) Only check that it runs without crashing
D) Ensure all files are in one directory
